elements.sent-2411-001: In mergoassociation, two atoms in separate optical traps are combined to form a molecule when the traps are merged.
elements.sent-2411-002: Previous theoretical treatments have considered only the relative motion of the atoms, neglecting coupling to the motion of the center of mass.
elements.sent-2411-003: We develop a theoretical method to include the coupling to center-of-mass motion and consider its consequences for experiments for both weak and strong coupling.
elements.sent-2411-004: We consider the example of RbCs and then extend the treatment to other systems where mergoassociation may be effective, namely RbSr, RbYb and CsYb.
elements.sent-2411-005: We consider the role of the coupling when the traps are anisotropic and the potential use of moveable traps to construct quantum logic gates.
elements.sent-2411-006: Recent experiments \cite{Ruttley:2023 have shown that two ultracold atoms, confined in separate optical traps or tweezers, may combine to form a weakly bound molecule when the traps are merged.}
elements.sent-2411-007: The process occurs because the energies of high-lying molecular states cross the energy of the atom pair as a function of trap separation.
elements.sent-2411-008: Coupling between the atom-pair and molecular states generates an avoided crossing between the states.
elements.sent-2411-009: Atom pairs can thus be converted into molecules by adiabatic passage as the traps are merged.
elements.sent-2411-010: The process is known as mergoassociation and has great potential for creating ultracold molecules that are inaccessible with other methods.
elements.sent-2411-011: The levels involved are shown schematically in Fig.\ \ref{fig:intro .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.43\textwidth]{mergo-com-schematic.pdf} \caption{Schematic representation of the energy levels involved in mergoassociation, as a function of trap separation $z_0$. The molecular level (approximately quadratic as a function of $z_0$) has avoided crossings with motional states of the atom pair (approximately horizontal at large $z_0$). Mergoassociation occurs when an atom pair in the lowest motional state is transferred into the molecular state by adiabatic passage over the lowest avoided crossing. The levels shown neglect motion of the center of mass. \label{fig:intro} } \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-012: The levels produced when two traps merge were first studied by Stock et al.\ \cite{Stock:2003, Stock:2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: They considered two atoms that are identically trapped.
elements.sent-2411-014: Under these circumstances, with harmonic traps, there is an exact separation of the motions in the relative and center-of-mass coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-015: Their calculations dealt entirely with the relative motion and with spherical traps.
elements.sent-2411-016: Following the experimental work \cite{Ruttley:2023 , which was carried out with Rb and Cs atoms in nonidentical optical tweezers with large anisotropy, we extended the formal theory to handle nonidentical, anisotropic traps \cite{Bird:mergo:2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, the numerical calculations presented in Ref.\ \cite{Bird:mergo:2023 were still limited to the relative motion, neglecting coupling to the motion of the center of mass.}
elements.sent-2411-018: The purpose of the present paper is to investigate the influence of center-of-mass motion on the energy levels involved in mergoassociation and to consider their implications for experiments.
elements.sent-2411-019: Idziaszek et al.\ \cite{Idziaszek:2007 briefly considered the coupling between the relative and center-of-mass motions for atom-ion interactions in one dimension and commented that certain avoided crossings were weaker.}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, the problem has not been considered in 3 dimensions and the dependence of the level patterns on the coupling strength has not been explored.
elements.sent-2411-021: The influence of coupling between the relative and center-of-mass motions on the levels that arise for two interacting particles in a single trap has been studied more extensively \cite{Deuretzbacher:2008, Mentink:2009, Jachymski:trap:2013, Jachymski:2016, Jachymski:2020, Hood:NaCs:2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: The structure of this paper is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-023: Section \ref{sec:theory introduces the problem and describes the methods we use, including an important modification of the molecular basis functions that dramatically improves convergence.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Section \ref{sec:effects uses the example of RbCs to explore the effects of the coupling between relative and center-of-mass motions for both weak and strong coupling (Sections \ref{sec:weak and \ref{sec:strong ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: We consider the consequences of the coupling for mergoassociation starting from atoms either in their motional ground states or in motionally excited states.
elements.sent-2411-026: This section also explores mergoassociation for other systems, considering the examples of RbSr, RbYb and CsYb (Section \ref{sec:other-systems ), the effect of the strong anisotropy of the tweezer traps used in current experiments (Section \ref{sec:aniso ), and the potential use of moveable traps to construct quantum logic gates (Section \ref{sec:logic ).}}}

elements.sent-2411-143: Mergoassociation is potentially useful for many systems.

elements.sent-2411-174: Merging traps may also have applications in quantum information processing \cite{Stock:2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-175: The interactions that control the energy levels depend on the hyperfine state of the atoms involved, so they may be used to accumulate phase differences between pairs of atoms in different states.
elements.sent-2411-176: This allows the production of controlled entanglement and the construction of 2-particle quantum-logic gates.
elements.sent-2411-177: An important general insight from the present work is that, for a particular mass ratio $m_2/m_1$, the patterns of levels are ``universal" when lengths are expressed in terms of the scattering length and energies (and frequencies) are expressed in terms of the energy of the least-bound molecular state.
elements.sent-2411-178: Thus the key requirement for achieving differential phase shifts is that the scattering lengths are significantly different for the different pairs of atomic states involved.
elements.sent-2411-179: This is satisfied for most alkali-metal pairs, but not for all; modeling it requires a good understanding of the interaction potentials and detailed coupled-channel calculations using them \cite{Julienne:FD142, Takekoshi:RbCs:2012, Groebner:KCs:2017, Brookes:2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-180: The presence of coupling between relative and center-of-mass motions is a complicating factor for applications to logic gates.
elements.sent-2411-181: At the simplest level, such coupling modifies the trap separation at which the principal avoided crossing occurs, as described by Eq.\ \ref{eq:mol-shift .}
elements.sent-2411-182: It is important to take this into account.
elements.sent-2411-183: Nevertheless, for interactions involving pairs of atoms in their motional ground states, this is simply a quantitative correction.
elements.sent-2411-184: Another issue is the feasibility (or fidelity) of quantum logic operations at finite temperature, when not all atoms are in their motional ground states.
elements.sent-2411-185: In this context, it would be desirable if the potential curves for motionally excited atoms were parallel to those for ground-state atoms.
elements.sent-2411-186: This occurs when the trapping frequencies for the two atoms are exactly equal, but not when the difference between them is significant.
elements.sent-2411-187: One possible advantage arises in cases where the difference is very small: then, as seen in Fig.\ \ref{fig:weak (b), the potential curve for an atom excited in the higher motional frequency is very similar to that for the absolute ground state, while that for an atom excited in the lower frequency is not.}
elements.sent-2411-188: Thus, if one atom is less well cooled that the other, it may be helpful to ensure that its trapping frequency is slightly (but as little as possible) higher than that of its companion.
elements.sent-2411-189: We have developed theoretical methods to calculate the energies of pairs of atoms in separated optical traps, taking account of both trap anisotropy and the coupling between relative and center-of-mass motions.
elements.sent-2411-190: The resulting levels are important both for molecule formation by mergoassociation and for potential applications in quantum information processing.
elements.sent-2411-191: We use basis sets based on Cartesian harmonic-oscillator functions for both relative and center-of-mass motion.
elements.sent-2411-192: The functions for relative motion are supplemented with a single molecular function.
elements.sent-2411-193: The effective trap potential for center-of-mass motion that is felt by the molecular function is shifted from the minimum of the combined trap; taking account of this shift complicates the algebra, but produces a substantial reduction in the size of the basis set needed for convergence.
elements.sent-2411-194: Both mergoassociation and applications to quantum-logic gates rely on adiabatic passage over avoided crossings between atom-pair states and molecular states as a function of trap separation.
elements.sent-2411-195: The strengths of these avoided crossings are thus particularly important.
elements.sent-2411-196: We have used the example of RbCs to explore the dependence of the level patterns and the crossing strengths on the frequency difference between the traps for the two atoms.
elements.sent-2411-197: The lowest crossing, which is crucial for both applications, shifts to larger trap separations and becomes significantly weaker when center-of-mass motion is accounted for.
elements.sent-2411-198: Other crossings, which are important when merging traps containing motionally excited atoms, show more complicated behavior.
elements.sent-2411-199: We have extended our treatment to other systems.
elements.sent-2411-200: Mergoassociation is generally feasible for atom pairs with positive scattering lengths that are comparable to or larger than the harmonic lengths of the traps.
elements.sent-2411-201: This corresponds to binding energies (for the least-bound state) that are not more than a few times the trap frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-202: We have considered RbSr, RbYb and CsYb, which are resistant to magnetoassociation because their Feshbach resonances are so narrow and so sparse.
elements.sent-2411-203: All three systems have isotopic combinations with large positive scattering lengths.
elements.sent-2411-204: We have shown that, in units scaled by scattering lengths and binding energies, the level crossing diagrams are very similar for all three systems when the scaled trap freqencies are the same; they differ only because the ratio of atomic masses differs between systems.
elements.sent-2411-205: For all three systems, mergoassociation can form Feshbach molecules in the least-bound state with experimentally accessible trap frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-206: Optical tweezer traps are usually strongly anisotropic, with much stronger confinement across the laser beam waist than along the beam.
elements.sent-2411-207: We have considered the combined effects of anisotropy and coupling between relative and center-of-mass motions for RbCs, using trap frequencies typical of current experiments.
elements.sent-2411-208: We have found that the effect of anisotropy is weaker than that of motional coupling under these conditions.
elements.sent-2411-209: We have also explored the effect of motional coupling on the levels that might be used for quantum-logic gates.
elements.sent-2411-210: We have found that coupling between relative and center-of-mass motions can have substantial effects on the energy levels of separated traps.
elements.sent-2411-211: When merging traps containing atoms that are both in their motional ground states, the coupling leaves the general picture unchanged, but has significant effects that should be taken into account in quantitative work.
elements.sent-2411-212: However, when one or both atoms is in a motionally excited state, the coupling causes qualitative changes in the patterns of energy levels, which have important consequences for experimental outcomes.

elements.subsection-2411-010: Coupling between relative and center-of-mass motions
elements.sent-2411-233: The matrix elements of $V_cpl^trap(\boldsymbol{R ,\mathbfcal{R )$ may be factorized \begin{equation}\langle n'_x n'_y n'_z N'_X N'_Y N'_Z | V_cpl^trap(R,\mathbfcal{R}) | n_x n_y n_z N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle = \mu \langle n'_x n'_y n'_z | (R - R_0)^\intercal | n_x n_y n_z \rangle \Delta\omega^2 \langle N'_X N'_Y N'_Z | \mathbfcal{R} - \mathbfcal{R}_0 | N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle \label{eq:cpl-t-t} \langle a N'_X N'_Y N'_Z | V_cpl^trap(R,\mathbfcal{R}) | a N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle = \mu \langle a | (R - R_0)^\intercal | a \rangle \Delta\omega^2 \langle N'_X N'_Y N'_Z | \mathbfcal{R} - \mathbfcal{R}_0 | N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle; \label{eq:cpl-a-a} \langle a N'_X N'_Y N'_Z | V_cpl^trap(R,\mathbfcal{R}) | n_x n_y n_z N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle = \mu \langle a | (R - R_0)^\intercal | n_x n_y n_z \rangle \Delta\omega^2 \langle N'_X N'_Y N'_Z | \mathbfcal{R} - \mathbfcal{R}_0 | N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle, \label{eq:cpl-a-t}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\langle a | R - R_0 | a \rangle = -R_0.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-024: \begin{equation}\langle n'_x n'_y n'_z N'_X N'_Y N'_Z | V_cpl^trap(R,\mathbfcal{R}) | n_x n_y n_z N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle = \mu \langle n'_x n'_y n'_z | (R - R_0)^\intercal | n_x n_y n_z \rangle \Delta\omega^2 \langle N'_X N'_Y N'_Z | \mathbfcal{R} - \mathbfcal{R}_0 | N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle \label{eq:cpl-t-t} \langle a N'_X N'_Y N'_Z | V_cpl^trap(R,\mathbfcal{R}) | a N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle = \mu \langle a | (R - R_0)^\intercal | a \rangle \Delta\omega^2 \langle N'_X N'_Y N'_Z | \mathbfcal{R} - \mathbfcal{R}_0 | N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle; \label{eq:cpl-a-a} \langle a N'_X N'_Y N'_Z | V_cpl^trap(R,\mathbfcal{R}) | n_x n_y n_z N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle = \mu \langle a | (R - R_0)^\intercal | n_x n_y n_z \rangle \Delta\omega^2 \langle N'_X N'_Y N'_Z | \mathbfcal{R} - \mathbfcal{R}_0 | N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle, \label{eq:cpl-a-t}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-234: Matrix elements involving $(\boldsymbol{R - \boldsymbol{R _0) | n_x n_y n_z \rangle$ are evaluated using the identity \begin{equation}(z - z_0) | n_z \rangle = 2^{-\frac{1}{2}} \beta_{rel,z} \left( \sqrt{n_z}\, | n_z-1 \rangle + \sqrt{n_z+1}\, | n_z+1 \rangle \right),\end{equation} and similarly for other components.}}
elements.sent-2411-235: Thus \begin{equation}\langle a | z - z_0 | n_x n_y n_z \rangle = 2^{-\frac{1}{2}} \beta_{rel,z} \left(\sqrt{n_z}\, S_{a,n_xn_yn_z-1} + \sqrt{n_z+1}\, S_{a,n_xn_yn_z+1}\right) \langle n'_x n'_y n'_z | z - z_0 | n_x n_y n_z \rangle = \delta_{n'_x n_x} \delta_{n'_y n_y} 2^{-\frac{1}{2}} \beta_{rel,z} \left( \delta_{n'_z,n_z-1} \sqrt{n_z} + \delta_{n'_z,n_z+1} \sqrt{n_z+1} \, \right), \label{eq:zpzz}\end{equation} with similar expressions for $x-x_0$, $y-y_0$.
elements.formula-2411-027: \begin{equation}\langle a | z - z_0 | n_x n_y n_z \rangle = 2^{-\frac{1}{2}} \beta_{rel,z} \left(\sqrt{n_z}\, S_{a,n_xn_yn_z-1} + \sqrt{n_z+1}\, S_{a,n_xn_yn_z+1}\right) \langle n'_x n'_y n'_z | z - z_0 | n_x n_y n_z \rangle = \delta_{n'_x n_x} \delta_{n'_y n_y} 2^{-\frac{1}{2}} \beta_{rel,z} \left( \delta_{n'_z,n_z-1} \sqrt{n_z} + \delta_{n'_z,n_z+1} \sqrt{n_z+1} \, \right), \label{eq:zpzz}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-236: For the center-of-mass coordinates, the analogous expressions are \begin{equation}\langle N'_X N'_Y N'_Z | Z - Z_0 | N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle = \delta_{N'_X N_X} \delta_{N'_Y N_Y} 2^{-\frac{1}{2}} \beta_{com,Z} \left( \delta_{N'_Z,N_Z-1} \sqrt{N_Z} + \delta_{N'_Z,N_Z+1} \sqrt{N_Z+1} \, \right), \label{eq:ZpZZ}\end{equation} and similarly for $X-X_0$ and $Y-Y_0$.
elements.formula-2411-028: \begin{equation}\langle N'_X N'_Y N'_Z | Z - Z_0 | N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle = \delta_{N'_X N_X} \delta_{N'_Y N_Y} 2^{-\frac{1}{2}} \beta_{com,Z} \left( \delta_{N'_Z,N_Z-1} \sqrt{N_Z} + \delta_{N'_Z,N_Z+1} \sqrt{N_Z+1} \, \right), \label{eq:ZpZZ}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-237: For the shifted-molecule basis set, the center-of-mass functions $|\tilde{N _X \tilde{N _Y \tilde{N _Z \rangle$ are shifted in $\mathbfcal{R}$ for functions containing $|a\rangle$ but not for those containing $| n_x n_y n_z \rangle$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-238: This leaves Eqs.\ \ref{eq:com-t-t and \ref{eq:cpl-t-t unchanged, but Eqs.\ \ref{eq:com-a-a and \ref{eq:cpl-a-a are replaced by \begin{equation}\langle a \tilde{N}'_X \tilde{N}'_Y \tilde{N}'_Z | \hat{H}_com^trap(\mathbfcal{R}) + V_cpl^trap(R,\mathbfcal{R}) | a \tilde{N}_X \tilde{N}_Y \tilde{N}_Z \rangle = \left( E_{\tilde{N}_X \tilde{N}_Y \tilde{N}_Z} - \frac{\mu}{2} R_0^\intercal \Delta\omega^2 \Delta\mathbfcal{R} \right) \delta_{\tilde{N}'_X \tilde{N}_X} \delta_{\tilde{N}'_Y \tilde{N}_Y} \delta_{\tilde{N}'_Z \tilde{N}_Z}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-239: The matrix elements (\ref{eq:com-a-t ) and (\ref{eq:cpl-a-t ) are also modified because $|N_X N_Y N_Z\rangle$ and $|\tilde{N _X \tilde{N _Y \tilde{N _Z\rangle$ are nonorthogonal, \begin{equation}\langle a \tilde{N}'_X \tilde{N}'_Y \tilde{N}'_Z | \hat{H}_com^trap(\mathbfcal{R}) | n_x n_y n_z N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle = E_{N_X N_Y N_Z} S_{a,n_x n_y n_z} \langle \tilde{N}'_X | N_X \rangle \langle \tilde{N}'_Y | N_Y \rangle \langle \tilde{N}'_Z | N_Z \rangle; \label{eq:com-mod-a-t} \langle a \tilde{N}'_X \tilde{N}'_Y \tilde{N}'_Z | V_cpl^trap(\mathbfcal{R}) | n_x n_y n_z N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle = \mu \langle a | (R - R_0)^\intercal | n_x n_y n_z \rangle \Delta\omega^2 \langle \tilde{N}'_X \tilde{N}'_Y \tilde{N}'_Z | \mathbfcal{R} - \mathbfcal{R}_0 | N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle, \label{eq:cpl-mod-a-t}\end{equation} where the overlap integrals between shifted and unshifted functions along each Cartesian axis $\alpha$ are \cite{Gradshteyn:2015 \begin{equation}\langle \tilde{m} | n \rangle = \left(\frac{m!}{2^{n-m} n!}\right)^\frac{1}{2} \rho_\alpha^{n-m} L_m^{n-m}(\rho_\alpha^2/2) \exp(-\rho_\alpha^2/4). \label{eq:mod-overlap}\end{equation} Here $\rho_\alpha=\Delta\mathcal{R _\alpha/\beta_{com,\alpha}$, $n\ge m$ and $L_m^{n-m}$ is an associated Laguerre polynomial.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-030: \begin{equation}\langle a \tilde{N}'_X \tilde{N}'_Y \tilde{N}'_Z | \hat{H}_com^trap(\mathbfcal{R}) | n_x n_y n_z N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle = E_{N_X N_Y N_Z} S_{a,n_x n_y n_z} \langle \tilde{N}'_X | N_X \rangle \langle \tilde{N}'_Y | N_Y \rangle \langle \tilde{N}'_Z | N_Z \rangle; \label{eq:com-mod-a-t} \langle a \tilde{N}'_X \tilde{N}'_Y \tilde{N}'_Z | V_cpl^trap(\mathbfcal{R}) | n_x n_y n_z N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle = \mu \langle a | (R - R_0)^\intercal | n_x n_y n_z \rangle \Delta\omega^2 \langle \tilde{N}'_X \tilde{N}'_Y \tilde{N}'_Z | \mathbfcal{R} - \mathbfcal{R}_0 | N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle, \label{eq:cpl-mod-a-t}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-240: The matrix elements of $\mathbfcal{R - \mathbfcal{R _0$ in Eq.\ \ref{eq:cpl-mod-a-t are expressed in terms of their Cartesian components, \begin{equation}\langle \tilde{N}'_X \tilde{N}'_Y \tilde{N}'_Z | Z - Z_0 | N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle = \langle \tilde{N}'_X | N_X \rangle \langle \tilde{N}'_Y | N_Y \rangle 2^{-\frac{1}{2}} \beta_{com,Z} \left( \langle \tilde{N}'_Z | N_Z-1 \rangle \sqrt{N_Z} + \langle \tilde{N}'_Z | N_Z+1 \rangle \sqrt{N_Z+1} \,\right)\end{equation} and similarly for $X-X_0$ and $Y-Y_0$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-241: Finally, the non-zero off-diagonal elements of the overlap matrix are \begin{equation}\langle a \tilde{N}'_X \tilde{N}'_Y \tilde{N}'_Z | n_x n_y n_z N_X N_Y N_Z \rangle = S_{a, n_x n_y n_z} \langle \tilde{N}'_X | N_X \rangle \langle \tilde{N}'_Y | N_Y \rangle \langle \tilde{N}'_Z | N_Z \rangle.\end{equation}. \end{widetext.}
